hook: Almond-blossom estate that turns Madrid pink in February
facts: Laid out in the 1920s for engineer Cesareo Iradier on a former farm.
Some 1,500 almond trees flower in February and March before the rest of the city wakes.
Named for old windmills that once pumped its irrigation water.
Run free by the city council, near Suanzes metro.
w: Every February the almond trees here bloom a week before anyone expects spring, and half of Madrid quietly defects east to photograph the pink. An engineer built it in the 1920s, naming it for the windmills that once worked the water. The rest of the year, near-empty. Time it right.

hook: Walled 18th-century garden hidden below Plaza de la Paja
facts: Designed around 1761 on a Chalmandrier plan, the last surviving palace garden of its era in the centre.
Sits on an artificial embankment bridging Plaza de la Paja and Calle Segovia.
Reconstructed in 1987, opened to the public in 2002.
Roughly 500 square metres behind a high brick wall.
w: Tucked behind a brick wall off Plaza de la Paja, this neoclassical patch survives as the last palace garden of its kind in old Madrid. Box hedges, a stone fountain, a rose pergola, and a wall thick enough to mute the city entirely. Laid out around 1761. Easy to walk straight past.

hook: Romantic ducal garden hiding a Civil War command bunker
facts: Commissioned by the Duchess of Osuna and laid out between 1787 and 1839.
Madrid's only garden in the Romantic style, with a French parterre and an English landscape section.
A 2,000-square-metre bunker 15 metres down served as Republican army HQ from 1937.
Open only Saturdays, Sundays and holidays; bunker by booking.
w: A duchess built her whim out here in the 1780s, complete with hermit's grotto and a temple to Bacchus. Then in 1937 the Republican army sank a bunker fifteen metres beneath the rose beds and ran the central front from it. Romance over a war room. Open weekends only, which keeps the crowds thin.

hook: Guinness-certified oldest restaurant on Earth, roasting since 1725
facts: Opened as an inn by Jean Botin and his wife in 1725, run continuously since.
Guinness World Records lists it as the world's oldest restaurant.
Cochinillo roasts in the original wood-fired oven, never let to go cold.
Hemingway closes 'The Sun Also Rises' at one of its upstairs tables.
legend: They say Goya washed dishes here as a young man before he could paint.
w: Three hundred years and that oven has never gone fully cold, not through civil war, not through lockdown. Founded 1725, certified by Guinness as the oldest restaurant going, it roasts suckling pig in a cave-like cellar. Hemingway ended 'The Sun Also Rises' upstairs. Legend says a young Goya scrubbed its dishes.

hook: Ninth-century Moorish wall, the oldest standing thing in Madrid
facts: Built in the 9th century under Emirate rule, defending the riverside fortress of Mayrit.
The flint stretch in Parque Emir Mohamed I runs roughly 120 metres with surviving towers.
Predates the founding of the Christian city by centuries.
Free, open-air, beside the Almudena cathedral on Cuesta de la Vega.
w: Madrid began as Mayrit, an Arab river fort, and this flint rampart on Cuesta de la Vega remains the oldest thing standing in the city. Ninth century, raised under the Cordoban emirs, with stub towers still leaning over a sunken park. Everyone files past to the cathedral and misses the actual origin of the place.

hook: Cavernous neo-Romanesque crypt beneath the cathedral, 400 columns
facts: Inaugurated in 1911, decades before the cathedral above was finished in 1993.
Over 400 columns, each capital carved differently.
Latin-cross plan with an ambulatory ringed by eighteen chapels.
Free entry under the Almudena, far emptier than the church above.
w: The crypt opened in 1911; the cathedral on top of it limped to completion only in 1993. So the basement aged eighty years ahead of its own roof. Four hundred columns, no two capitals alike, vaults running off into shadow. Most visitors crane up at the church and never go down. Their loss.

hook: Bronze of Vallecas's heavy-metal grandmother throwing the horns
facts: Honours Angeles Rodriguez Hidalgo (1900-1993), Vallecas's beloved 'rock granny'.
Unveiled in 1994, sculpted by Carmen Jorba in granite and bronze.
She appeared on the cover of Panzer's 1984 album 'Toca Madera'.
Stands on the boulevard of Calle Pena Gorbea.
w: Angeles Rodriguez worked as a maid, widowed at 41 with five children, and in her eighties turned up at every heavy-metal gig in Vallecas, leather jacket and studded wristband, throwing the horns. The neighbourhood loved her enough to cast her in bronze in 1994. Spiked cuff, raised fist, eternal headbang.

hook: Madrid's oldest square, three eras around one cobbled yard
facts: Among the oldest squares in the city, a medieval civic hub off Calle Mayor.
The 15th-century Mudejar Torre de los Lujanes ranks among Madrid's oldest buildings.
Plateresque Casa de Cisneros dates to 1537; the old town hall sits opposite.
Tradition holds Francois I of France was held in the tower after Pavia in 1525.
legend: According to tradition, the captured French king Francois I was lodged in the Torre de los Lujanes after the Battle of Pavia.
w: Three centuries stand around one small cobbled yard: a Mudejar tower from the 1400s, a Plateresque mansion from 1537, an old town hall facing it. Tradition holds the captured French king Francois I was locked in that tower after Pavia. The medieval heart of Madrid, a minute off the tourist drag.

hook: Glass-and-iron greenhouse in an old slaughterhouse by the river
facts: Built 1908-1928 by Luis Bellido as part of the municipal slaughterhouse complex.
Restored as a greenhouse in 1992, now spanning around 7,100 square metres.
Four enclosed microclimates: two tropical, one subtropical, one desert.
Free, in Madrid Rio beside the Manzanares.
w: Everyone knows the Crystal Palace in the Retiro; almost nobody knows this one, a glass hangar wedged into the old city slaughterhouse by the river. Built for livestock around 1910, replanted in 1992 with four climates from desert to rainforest under one iron roof. Free, warm, and reliably deserted.

hook: A genuine 2,200-year-old Egyptian temple, reassembled in a Madrid park
facts: Erected in the early 2nd century BC near Aswan, dedicated to Amun and Isis.
Egypt gave it to Spain in 1968 for help saving Nubian monuments from the Aswan dam.
Shipped stone by stone and rebuilt in Parque del Oeste, opened 1972.
The only ancient Egyptian temple in Spain.
w: Egypt gave Spain a temple. When the Aswan dam threatened to drown Nubia, Madrid helped salvage monuments and was thanked in 1968 with this 2,200-year-old chapel to Amun, dismantled stone by stone and rebuilt in a park. Reflected in its pool at sunset, it ranks among the city's strangest skylines.

n: Cafe del Jardin, Museo del Romanticismo
hook: Tearoom in a Romantic-era palace garden behind Malasana
facts: Set in the magnolia garden of the Museo del Romanticismo on Calle San Mateo.
The museum occupies a late-18th-century palace evoking 19th-century Madrid.
Period fountain, wooden benches, homemade tarts; no museum ticket needed for the cafe.
Tuesday to Sunday, hours shifting with the season.
w: Behind a museum devoted to Madrid's Romantic age sits its best-kept secret: a walled magnolia garden with a period fountain, a handful of tables, and homemade tart. You can skip the ticket and just take coffee among the ghosts of 1840s parlour life. Three streets off Malasana, and almost silent.

hook: Madrid's last grand Basque pelota court, neo-Mudejar and reborn
facts: Built 1891-1894 by Joaquin Rucoba, seating up to 4,000 for pelota.
The last surviving historic fronton in Madrid.
Last match played in 1919; decades of decay and demolition threats followed.
Declared a cultural monument in 2011, restored, opened to visitors free in 2024.
w: Tiered galleries of brick and iron wrap a long stone court where crowds of 4,000 once roared at Basque pelota. Built in 1894, abandoned after 1919, it spent a century rotting under demolition orders before the city finally saved it. Reopened free in 2024. The last great fronton in Madrid, and it nearly went.

hook: Western Europe's largest cemetery, a city of five million dead
facts: Created in 1884 after a cholera epidemic, officially opened in 1925.
Among the largest cemeteries in Western Europe, with an estimated five million burials.
Houses Spain's first crematorium, opened 1973.
Separate civil, Hebrew and Catholic grounds; ornate 19th-century pantheons.
w: Cholera forced Madrid to bury its dead out here in 1884, and the place never stopped growing: five million graves, said to outnumber the living city. Marble angels and tiny mausoleum-houses line the old avenues, while poorer dead stack in cliff-like niche walls. Vast, quiet, and easy to get lost in. Bring a map.

hook: Speakeasy club hidden behind a fake haberdashery on Tirso de Molina
facts: Fronted by a sham stocking-and-notions shop on Plaza de Tirso de Molina.
Created by the team behind the cabaret show 'The Hole'.
Three dance floors and cabaret turns every few minutes for up to a thousand.
Open late, Fridays and Saturdays; entry ticketed.
w: From the square it reads as a dusty old hosiery shop, except no haberdashery opens at midnight and shuts at five. Push through and the back wall gives onto three dance floors and cabaret acrobats dropping from the ceiling every quarter hour. The cabaret team behind 'The Hole' runs it. Pure theatrical sleight of hand.

hook: A marquess's mansion frozen exactly as he left it in 1922
facts: The former home of the 17th Marquess of Cerralbo, a collector and politician.
Bequeathed to the state and opened as a museum largely as the family lived in it.
Crammed ballroom, armoury, chandeliers and mirrors of late-19th-century taste.
On Calle Ventura Rodriguez near Plaza de Espana.
w: The Marquess of Cerralbo crammed his palace with armour, paintings and gilt mirrors, then willed it to the nation on the condition nothing be rearranged. So it stands as he left it: a maximalist aristocrat's home, mirrored ballroom and all, untouched since the 1920s. The richest house in Madrid that nobody visits.

hook: Cloistered nuns selling cookies through a wooden turnstile
facts: Founded in 1607 by the widowed countess and nun Beatriz Ramirez de Mendoza.
Named for a Virgin painting found in a coalyard (carboneria).
Enclosed Hieronymite nuns sell sweets via a lazy-susan turnstile, unseen.
Tucked on Calle del Codo behind Plaza de la Villa.
w: Ring the bell marked 'Monjas', follow the signs to the 'torno', and order cookies from cloistered nuns you will never see, money and biscuits exchanged on a rotating wooden drum. The community has held here since 1607, named for a Virgin found in a coalyard. Down the crook of Calle del Codo. Bring small change.
